name: M.S. in Architecture

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: Only three courses are referenced (Urban Design Studio I, History and Theory of Urban Planning and Design, and GIS), all at depth 0, which is too thin to show a coherent post-professional M.S. trajectory. The three that do appear lean urban/planning, which reads oddly for a generically titled 'M.S. in Architecture' and suggests the plan of study is under-parsed or under-specified rather than deliberately narrow.
dimensions.suggestion: Populate the catalog entry with the full core-plus-elective structure and a required research-methods and thesis sequence so the degree's intended concentration and progression are visible.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 0 and none of the three courses lists a prerequisite, so there is no visible structure or gating at all; missingCourseCount is 0 only because so few courses are referenced. For a graduate research degree a completely flat graph offers no evidence of sequencing.
dimensions.suggestion: Introduce a research-methods prerequisite ahead of thesis/independent-study credits and record electives so a minimal but real prerequisite structure appears.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives are present, and with only three courses shown the page gives a prospective research student almost nothing to judge intended competencies against.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 program learning outcomes describing the research and design-inquiry capabilities the M.S. develops.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null with no parsed plan, so neither the total (an M.S. is typically ~30 credits) nor any split between core, electives, and thesis is transparent; creditReconcileDelta is uncomputable.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credit requirement and how it divides among required core, electives, and thesis/research credits.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The page is sparse: three courses, no plan of study, no total credits, and no labeled terms. The three descriptions that exist are readable, but the overall entry cannot orient a prospective student.
dimensions.suggestion: Rebuild the catalog page with a complete course list, plan of study, and credit summary.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The visible courses are individually current and marketable, GIS, urban design, and planning history/theory, and align with contemporary spatial-analysis and urbanism practice, but three courses are too few to assess whether the degree as a whole reflects the discipline's frontier.
dimensions.suggestion: Add contemporary electives (computational design, sustainability/resilience, urban analytics) and surface them so the degree's currency is demonstrable.

descriptionQuality.justification: The three descriptions present are clear and specific (e.g., GIS as a system for storing and manipulating spatial data; the planning course's interdisciplinary framing), but the sample is far too small to establish consistent description quality across the degree.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: No prerequisites are stated on any of the three courses; the entries are unambiguous and free of dangling references, but the total absence of prerequisites gives little to assess and no evidence of intended sequencing.

missingTopics.rationale: A research M.S. needs an explicit methods course (qualitative, quantitative, and design-based inquiry); none is visible among the three referenced courses.
No thesis, capstone, or independent-study course appears, leaving the culminating research experience undocumented for a degree whose value proposition is research.
Only GIS represents digital methods; parametric modeling and performance simulation are expected in a contemporary architecture M.S.
No environmental, energy, or resilience content is referenced, a significant omission for graduate architectural study today.
Given the urban lean of the visible courses, spatial statistics and data-driven urban analysis would round out the concentration.

emergingTrends.rationale: The GIS and urban-design courses position the degree to grow toward spatial data science and simulation, which is where urban research is heading.
Architecture research increasingly centers climate adaptation; the degree should build a resilience research strand.
Machine-learning tools for spatial analysis and generative design are becoming standard research instruments the program should incorporate.
The planning-history course's landscape/geography framing signals an interdisciplinary direction the M.S. could formalize into a coherent research agenda.

emergingTrends.trend: Equity and social impact in the built environment
emergingTrends.rationale: Contemporary architecture research foregrounds spatial justice; an explicit equity strand would align the degree with the field's priorities.

topSuggestions: Rebuild the catalog entry with the complete core, elective, and thesis structure, since three referenced courses cannot represent a graduate degree.
Add and surface a required research-methods course and a thesis/independent-research vehicle.
State total credits and the core/elective/thesis credit split to fix the null credit transparency.
Publish program-level learning outcomes describing the research competencies developed.
Broaden and modernize electives (computational design, sustainability, urban analytics) and display them to demonstrate currency.
